story:
Barry Lewis, a time-traveling history student, “faxes” into the past and slips undetected behind a forest ridge to join the 22nd New York Volunteer Infantry during the Civil War. He’s exhilarated: columns of soldiers, supply trains, the scale of it all. He sends one-way V-mail updates to his professor via a cerebral uplink chip, awed by how easily he can think a message and transmit it across centuries. He trusts the invisible safety grid wrapped around him to stop bullets and germs, and he’s scheduled to be faxed back later, per RE-TRIP’s rules.

Marching grinds him down. He marvels at the gap between their world and his, even as he praises his own era’s conveniences. At camp, he witnesses disease up close: cholera and whooping cough rattle the ranks. A boy his age is declared fit for duty by a harried surgeon and sent away without so much as a decent pain draught or proper rest. Barry, protected by his safety grid, feels guilty and helpless. With one duroscan and a retinax injection, a doctor from his time could probably save the kid. But he reminds himself he’s only a witness. History has already happened.

The unit moves south of Sharpsburg. A narrow-path skirmish becomes Barry’s first taste of battle. Two men fall beside him. The screams are like nothing he’s ever read. His safety grid deflects the Minie balls, and he is forced to lie still, pretending to be hit as chaos spins around him. One soldier dies; another loses a leg. The next day, fighting intensifies. Barry’s meter sputters. He asks the professor to contact RE-TRIP. The light turns red. He tries to believe it’s fine, that this is normal.

It isn’t.

The grid crashes.

Now every cough, every bullet, every blade of weather can touch him. His scheduled return is still a week and a half away. RE-TRIP’s policy is strict: for an emergency fax-back, he must be alone so natives to the timeline won’t witness his disappearance. But the regiment is watching for deserters. Guards tighten the camp. Barry can’t slip out. By the following days, after exposure without the grid, he starts coughing, feverish and afraid. He begs for an extraction anyway, insists he doesn’t care about the prime directive. He just wants to go home.

At last he darts into the woods at night, alone, whispering through his V-mail that this is the moment—no witnesses. Then he’s spotted. A guard’s rifle butt slams into his head. They court-martial him for desertion. He stands before a firing squad. In his final messages, Barry clings to policy the way he once clung to technology: if there’s no chance he survives, surely RE-TRIP will change the rules and pull him out, even if he’s in full view. He explains—and misunderstands—the logic: earlier, they could not risk an extraction seen by people from the past; it would contaminate the timeline. Now, in formation, he is as visible as a man can be. RE-TRIP will not pull him because too many eyes would see. The company preserves history by letting it run.

“Ready,” the sergeant calls.

“Aim.” Barry thinks he feels the pull home. His transmission breaks off mid-word.

The thrill of seeing history firsthand becomes the horror of becoming part of it: a lone traveler, a failed shield, a rigid policy that values the timeline over one life, and a voice that fades where the record of history demands it.
